title: When Will Tamil Nadu Break Free from Those Who Deceive It?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. The eight source pages were checked for the dated Assembly statements, quoted English reports, costs, health-programme figures, and page joins. A repeated title fragment and an embedded printed page header introduced by OCR were removed, and only visually clear OCR errors were repaired.
paragraphs: I had explained in detail the statements Chief Minister Jayalalithaa read under Rule 110 in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly over the past four years, and what became of the schemes announced through them. As though adding a second bolt to the door in response to Ottakoothar's song, at the very moment I wrote about this at length, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa hurried into the Assembly on 25 August 2015, rapidly read a statement under Rule 110 containing twenty-two announcements, including the “Amma Master Health Check-up Scheme,” waited while members of allied parties delivered their speeches of praise, and then left the House. Jayalalithaa comes to read a statement; she has no interest in taking part in any of the Assembly's other proceedings.

All twenty-two announcements made by the Chief Minister on 25 August 2015 concerned the Health and Family Welfare Department. The debate on that department's demand for grants had already been scheduled for 23 September 2015 during the current session. If the Chief Minister read all twenty-two announcements concerning that department on 25 August 2015, what purpose remains for the debate on its demand for grants? And why is there a Minister for that department at all?

In a statement read under Rule 110 in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 30 April 2013, the Chief Minister said: “Considering the traffic congestion at the Koyambedu Mofussil Bus Terminus, and considering that more than half the buses leaving from there travel to the southern districts, I am pleased to announce that a mofussil bus terminus for buses arriving from and departing for the southern districts will be established near Vandalur railway station, between the Southern Trunk Road and the Outer Ring Road. The new terminus at Vandalur will provide space for operating mofussil buses, space for operating city buses, parking, transport-workshop facilities, parking for two-wheelers and four-wheelers, restaurants required by the public, small shops, public toilets, and every other facility.” The announcement received prominent coverage in all the newspapers at the time.

Two years and four months have passed since Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced this scheme under Rule 110 in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly. Has the new Vandalur bus terminus arrived? Have all the facilities Jayalalithaa promised in that announcement appeared? No. Only anguish remains.

Reports said that farmers in the area opposed the Chief Minister's announcement from the outset. Dinamalar reported them as saying: “Many families cultivate one hundred acres of wet agricultural land adjoining the Vandalur-Walajabad road. The Government has already taken several acres for road widening and for establishing the Vandalur Zoological Park. When then Chief Minister M.G.R. opened the Vandalur park in 1985, he said that a great deal of land had already been acquired in Vandalur and that not one more parcel would be taken. But now Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has announced that a mofussil bus terminus will be established on agricultural land. To protect the farmers' livelihood, the terminus must be moved elsewhere.”

As soon as the Chief Minister read her Rule 110 statement announcing a new terminus at Vandalur, I asked what had become of the earlier plans for new bus termini at Velachery and Madhavaram. The Government did not reply, but Dinamalar of 14 May 2013 carried a report headlined, “Velachery Mofussil Bus Terminus Abandoned: CMDA's New Decision.”

On 15 May 2013, I asked: “The Chief Minister has read a Rule 110 statement announcing a mofussil bus terminus at Vandalur, and farmers in the area have opposed it by citing M.G.R.'s promise. What will the Tamil Nadu Government do? Will the Vandalur terminus be built despite the farmers' opposition? What is the fate of the Velachery and Madhavaram termini? When will the Chief Minister read explanations of these matters under another Rule 110 statement?”

Two years after I asked that question, an explanation of the Vandalur bus-terminus announcement was finally given on 26 August 2015, not by Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, but by the departmental Minister. His explanation appears not to have been published in any newspaper except the Indian Express dated 26 August 2015. Dinamalar's online edition carried a small portion of the reply, but its printed newspaper omitted it. The Minister's reply was as follows:

“The State Government has accorded administrative sanction for Rs. 376 crores to develop a mofussil bus terminus at Vandalur to decongest the CMBT at Koyambedu. Talks will be held with the land owners to acquire land, State Minister for Housing and Urban Development R. Vaithilingam said on Tuesday. Replying to the discussion on demand for grants for the department in the Assembly, the Minister said a bus terminus near Vandalur-Walajabad road near the Vandalur railway station was announced by Chief Minister J. Jayalalithaa. But the process of land acquisition was being delayed as the Centre's new land acquisition bill was awaiting Parliament approval.”
Minister Vaithilingam also told the Assembly that the lands in the area would be consolidated, a comprehensive plan prepared, and action taken to establish the bus terminus within it.

The English daily Express reported that the Minister gave this reply in the Assembly. Is Minister Vaithilingam now saying that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa made the 2013 announcement without considering any of the land-acquisition problems? He spoke as though he had not made the announcement, stressing that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa had made it. He then said the terminus could not materialise because the Central Government's new land-acquisition law remained pending. Is the Minister engaged in what people call “putting flowers around one's ears,” an attempt to fool us? Jayalalithaa announced the project in 2013. The land-acquisition legislation was delayed in Parliament in 2014-15. If the Minister says that legislation caused the delay in establishing the terminus, one is reminded of Udumalai Narayana Kavi's words: “Even if you tell a lie, make it fit, you worthless fellows!”

Only now, they say, will they speak directly to all the landowners! The Minister says so! Then what were they doing for these two years? Jayalalithaa announced in the Assembly under Rule 110 in 2013 that a mofussil bus terminus would be built at Vandalur, but only now, two years later, have they granted even administrative sanction. What a false announcement she made in the Assembly! Before this scheme was announced, when M.G.R. was Chief Minister, he had already declared that no more land in that area would be acquired. On what basis, then, did you announce in the Assembly that a Vandalur terminus would be established at an estimated cost of ₹376 crore?

They submitted such a false statement. Yet, speaking in the Assembly yesterday, Minister Vaithilingam said, “People are not prepared to trust Karunanidhi, who survives in politics by telling lies.” Is it not now clear who survives in politics through lies?

Was that all the Minister said in the Assembly? He came to reply to the debate on the demand for grants but criticised Dr Ramadoss and Anbumani Ramadoss as harshly as he possibly could. As for Opposition Leader Vijayakanth, the less said the better! In his reply, the Minister declared without the least civility: “Vijayakanth is staggering on the prohibition issue. Vijayakanth is one who can be destroyed through drink.” If such words are spoken in the Assembly, does it not raise the question whether what is taking place is truly a legislative session?

Just as with Vandalur, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa announced in a Rule 110 statement on 8 September 2011 that a satellite township would be created at Thirumazhisai for ₹2,160 crore. Four years were nearly over, and I asked where that satellite township was. The Minister answered that question in the Assembly, too. Did he reply by saying where the township had been built? No. Instead, he said: “Action is being taken to form a new satellite township with 12,000 residential flats at an estimated cost of Rs. 2,160 crores over an extent of 311.05 acres at Thirumazhisai.” In other words, action is supposedly being taken to create it.

Likewise, on 4 April 2013, Jayalalithaa read under Rule 110 that a satellite township would be developed near Madurai Airport at a cost of ₹120 crore. I had asked whether at least that scheme had been completed. Minister Vaithilingam's reply in the Assembly yesterday was: “The preparation of concept and theme based layout for a satellite township at Thoppur-Uchapatti in Madurai was under way.” In other words, the scheme remains at its opening stage. How many crores of the ₹120 crore have been spent? Why did the Minister not tell the Assembly? He could not, because a mere ₹7 lakh had been spent!

Udanpirappē, if this is the fate of schemes announced under Rule 110 three and four years ago, can we believe that there is any substance to the announcements Chief Minister Jayalalithaa now comes to the Assembly each day solely to read under Rule 110 before leaving?

The foremost among them is the announcement that an “Amma Master Health Check-up Scheme” will be launched. Is that a new scheme? No. It is a scheme already operating in hospitals, through which blood tests, cholesterol tests, cardiac examinations, and similar checks are conducted. Yet they have announced it as though it were a new discovery. Under the DMK Government itself, an order was issued on 11 June 1999 to introduce a master health check-up scheme in every medical-college hospital. If the Chief Minister has doubts, she may obtain the old Government Order from the Secretariat, read it, and find out.

And the Amma Master Health Check-up Scheme is supposedly for men! The “Amma Women's Special Master Health Check-up Scheme” is supposedly for women. Such is the announcement. If the same thing is done in a village, it is called the “Amma Arokkiya Scheme”! To say more, the DMK Government introduced the “Varumun Kappom” scheme in 1997 to detect diseases at an early stage in rural and urban areas and provide appropriate treatment.

The AIADMK Government that followed abandoned the scheme. It was revived under the DMK Government on 30 December 2006, and by 28 February 2011, 19,272 medical camps had been conducted throughout Tamil Nadu, benefiting 1,81,66,000 people. If they now behave as though such a scheme is being announced for the first time, does the department think the people of Tamil Nadu are innocent and know nothing, and seek to deceive them? Or, under compulsion to devise some supposedly new scheme that appears to praise Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, are they attaching names like these and staging a spectacle? The people who voted must understand.

More importantly, Jayalalithaa would do well to understand what kinds of false reports are being written for her and made into announcements in the Assembly. So long as newspapers exist to reproduce such announcements line by line, and some politicians exist to praise them to the skies, so long as there are people willing to be deceived, will there not always be deceivers? When will those being deceived mend their ways? When will Tamil Nadu break free from the grip of those who deceive it?
With affection,
M.K.
30 August 2015
